key_insight: An unsupervised machine-learning analysis of symptomatic U.S. adults found three distinct untreated mental-health subgroups that differ sharply by age, severity, and healthcare use.
clinical_relevance: The findings suggest the treatment gap is not uniform and may require life-stage-specific public health strategies to improve identification and care engagement.
method: Pooled 2015–2018 NHANES adult survey data (N=11,848) and isolated 3,075 adults with daily or weekly anxiety/depression symptoms.
Built a 22-feature matrix combining clinical biomarkers and social variables, then reduced it with Factor Analysis of Mixed Data (FAMD).
Used Gaussian Mixture Modelling (GMM), selected by Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), to define latent sub-populations.
evidence_signal: In the symptomatic cohort, 71.6% were entirely unmedicated, and the model separated three clusters with untreated rates ranging from 64.1% to 90.2%.
watch_item: Because this is cross-sectional survey data, the clusters describe associations rather than causal barriers to treatment and may not generalize beyond NHANES.
paper_title: Life-Stage Heterogeneity in the Mental Health Treatment Gap: An Unsupervised Machine Learning Profiling of Symptomatic US Adults
